title: Unwind a partially initialized controller on allocation failure
prompt:
The embedded controller factory in `src/controller.cpp` builds a transport, decoder, and controller in that order through a caller-supplied allocator. Its normal path works, but deterministic failure of a later allocation leaves an earlier transport object and its allocation live. Repair the allocation-failure unwind at its root.

Preserve the public API and exact initialization contract. Invalid configurations must return `init_error::invalid_configuration` without calling the allocator or lifecycle observer. Each valid attempt must allocate and construct transport, decoder, then controller in that order. If any allocation returns null, return `{nullptr, init_error::out_of_memory}`, destroy every object whose construction completed in strict reverse construction order, and return each successful allocation exactly once through the same allocator with its original pointer, size, and alignment. Never invoke a destructor for an allocation whose construction did not complete.

Keep the successful path allocation-free after its three initialization allocations: processing must retain the existing bias-then-scale result and sample counter, and shutdown must destroy and deallocate controller, decoder, then transport. Lifecycle notifications model resource acquisition and release and are part of the observable contract. Do not add global allocation, exceptions, ownership registries, dependencies, retries, fallback construction, or allocator-specific logic. Make the focused correction in `src/controller.cpp`. Do not modify the protected header, Makefile, or tests, and do not weaken the strict C++17 compiler flags. Run `make test`; it is offline and deterministic.

full_distill.primary_skill: repair deterministic multi-object allocation-failure unwind while preserving construction order, exact allocator symmetry, status causality, and the successful no-extra-allocation path
full_distill.baseline_evidence: the strict C++17 build succeeds and the successful controller path passes, but the deterministic allocator fixture exits nonzero when allocation two or three fails because the already-constructed transport is neither destroyed nor deallocated
full_distill.success_contract: all three injected allocation failures return out_of_memory and unwind only completed objects in reverse order with exact pointer/size/alignment deallocation; invalid input remains side-effect free; successful processing and reverse-order shutdown remain unchanged and perform no extra allocation
adversarial_factors: a fail-on-call allocator forces each of the three allocation sites without exceptions, randomness, host memory pressure, or timing
a shared audit log interleaves successful and failed allocation attempts with construction, destruction, and deallocation, pinning the exact unwind sequence
the allocator retains original pointer, size, and alignment records and requires strict LIFO, exactly-once release of every accepted block
lifecycle notifications distinguish releasing raw storage from actually destroying a resource-owning object, so deallocation alone cannot hide a skipped destructor
invalid configurations and successful processing pin error codes, zero-side-effect validation, bias-then-scale behavior, sample accounting, and the three-allocation fast path
